summary: 1925 silent Soviet film by Sergei Eisenstein dramatizing the 1905 mutiny aboard the Imperial Russian battleship Potemkin and the subsequent massacre of civilians on the Odessa Steps. Considered one of the greatest films ever made and a landmark of montage theory. Cinematography by Eduard Tisse.

works.name: Odessa Steps Sequence (Act IV)
works.description: The film's most famous and most-clued sequence, in which Tsarist Cossack soldiers descend the Odessa Steps massacring the civilian crowd below. Famous for the baby carriage, the nurse with broken pince-nez/glasses, and the legless man. Cited as a founding example of intellectual montage. Wikipedia

clues.clue: A baby carriage rolls down the Odessa Steps during the Cossack massacre of civilians
examples: a pram rolls down the Odessa steps in a Sergei Eisenstein film
In a montage in Act IV of this film, shots of a bleeding woman wearing broken glasses are interspersed with a baby carriage rolling down the Odessa Steps.

clues.clue: A nurse wearing pince-nez (broken glasses) is shot/shown bleeding from her eye; this image later inspired Francis Bacon's screaming-pope series
examples: montage to intercut close-ups of a screaming, bleeding woman wearing shattered glasses with shots of a rolling baby carriage
A still of a weeping nurse from this film inspired a Francis Bacon painting.

clues.clue: A legless man is seen crawling alongside the retreating crowd during the sequence
examples: A man without legs flees during an iconic 'intellectual montage' in Act IV of this film
this sequence, which begins after the caption 'suddenly...' interrupts a shot of a waving crowd that includes a legless man

clues.clue: Three separate shots of stone lion statues are edited together to create the illusion that the lions rise up in response to the massacre
examples: At its end, three separate shots of lion statues create the illusion that the lions are rising in response to the action of this sequence
An explosion in this film appears to cause a stone lion to jump up

clues.clue: Parodied in Terry Gilliam's Brazil: the baby carriage is replaced by a vacuum cleaner as Sam and Tuttle escape from the Ministry
examples: Terry Gilliam's Brazil pays homage to this film sequence as Sam and Tuttle escape from the Ministry, replacing a key object from this scene with a vacuum cleaner.
A classic scene from this film is parodied in a sequence involving a vacuum cleaner in Terry Gilliam's Brazil.

clues.clue: Brian De Palma homaged this scene in The Untouchables: a train station shootout in Chicago Union Station, with the pram rolling down stairs during Eliot Ness's gunfight
examples: The train station shootout from Brian de Palma's The Untouchables draws from this scene
To pay homage to this film, Brian De Palma moved a shootout scene from a hospital to Chicago Union Station in his movie The Untouchables.

clues.clue: A woman holding her trampled/shot son confronts the line of soldiers
examples: A woman holding a trampled baby faces down a line of soldiers in a scene from this film which exemplifies montage theory
Its best-known scene includes a mother carrying her wounded child back to the troops that shot him.

works.name: Mutiny / 'Men and Maggots' (Acts I–III)
works.description: The mutiny is sparked by sailors' refusal to eat borscht/soup made with maggot-infested meat. Sailor leader Vakulinchuk dies in the conflict and is laid in a tent on a pier. The doctor Smirnov is killed. A wild-haired priest thumps his cross into his palm. Soldiers cover sailors under a tarpaulin before being convinced not to fire. A plate inscribed 'Give us this day our daily bread' is smashed by a character who discovers the maggots. The only color in the film is on a red flag.

clues.clue: The mutiny is triggered by maggot-infested meat/borscht; sailors refuse to eat it
examples: The first section of this 1925 film is titled 'Men and Maggots' and shows Vakulinchuk leading a mutiny.
Men in this film go on a soup strike after discovering maggots have infected their meat.
central action is touched off by maggot-infested meat

clues.clue: A plate inscribed 'Give us this day our daily bread' is discovered/smashed after revealing the maggots
examples: The first section of this film ends with a soldier reading the inscription 'give us this day our daily bread' on a plate after being served meat infested with maggots.
While washing dishes, one character in this film reads 'Give us this day our daily bread' on a plate and elects to smash it.

clues.clue: Sailor leader Vakulinchuk dies and is laid in a tent on a pier
examples: Vakulinchuk is left in a tent on a pier after dying in this film's central conflict caused by maggot-covered meat.
Members of the Tsentralka organized one of these events during which Grigory Vakulinchuk smuggled a red flag.

clues.clue: A wild-haired priest bounces/thumps his cross into his palm; the doctor Smirnov is killed in that scene
examples: A priest in this film thumps his cross into his palm in a scene where the doctor Smirnov is killed.

This film cuts between shots of a wild-haired priest bouncing a cross on his hand and a group of soldiers aiming at other soldiers covered by a tarpaulin, who'd refused to say they liked the borscht they were given.

clues.clue: The only color in the entire silent film is on a red flag
examples: The only color in this film is on a red flag.

clues.clue: A man in the crowd shouts 'kill the Jews' before being beaten by the crowd gathered outside the tent
examples: In this film, a man shouts 'kill the Jews' before being beaten by a crowd gathered outside of a tent.

works.name: Director / Production / Legacy
works.description: Directed by Sergei Eisenstein; cinematography by Eduard Tisse. Commissioned by the Soviet government on the success of Eisenstein's earlier film Strike (1924). Made in 1925. The Berlin cut introduced Edmund Meisel's score. Considered a founding example of intellectual montage theory. Francis Bacon extensively referenced the screaming nurse image in his work. Chris Marker's A Grin Without a Cat opens with footage from the film.

clues.clue: Directed by Sergei Eisenstein; cinematography by Eduard Tisse; commissioned by the Soviet government after the success of Strike (1924)
examples: Name this classic film of Soviet cinema directed by Sergei Eisenstein that dramatizes a mutiny on the title vessel.
This film was commissioned on the success of the director's earlier film, Strike.

clues.clue: The Berlin cut introduced composer Edmund Meisel's score
examples: This film's Berlin cut introduced Edmund Meisel's score.

clues.clue: Eisenstein used five types of montage: intellectual, overtonal, tonal, rhythmic, and metric; worked with peers including Vsevolod Pudovkin
examples: This theorist used the terms 'intellectual,' 'overtonal,' 'tonal,' 'rhythmic,' and 'metric' to describe the five types of a concept he developed with peers like Vsevolod Pudovkin.

clues.clue: Francis Bacon extensively referenced the screaming/bleeding nurse image from the film — multiple works including 'Study for the Nurse in the Battleship Potemkin, 1957' and his screaming popes series
examples: A work by this artist titled 'Study for the Nurse in the Battleship Potemkin, 1957' is reminiscent of another work they modelled after a Diego Velazquez portrait of Pope Innocent X.
Many of Bacon's works were influenced by this film, such as a shot that depicts a nurse whose broken glasses pierce her bleeding eye.

clues.clue: Chris Marker's A Grin Without a Cat opens with a montage of shots from this film and found protest footage
examples: Chris Marker's A Grin Without a Cat opens with a montage of shots from this film and found protest footage.

clues.clue: The final shot shows the battleship charging directly at the viewer, appearing to split the screen
examples: its final shot shows this film's title object charging in at the viewer and appearing to split the screen in half.

clues.clue: A Radu Jude short film (The Potemkinists) features a sculptor struggling to make a canal-side monument to the film's protagonists inoffensive; another Radu Jude film (2022) freeze-frames the final scene of men waving hats
examples: A sculptor struggles to make a canal-side monument to this film's protagonists inoffensive in a Radu Jude short film.
This film's final scene of men waving and throwing their hats is freeze-framed in a 2022 Radu Jude film

comprehensive_summary:
Battleship Potemkin is a 1925 silent Soviet film directed by Sergei Eisenstein with cinematography by Eduard Tisse. It was commissioned by the Soviet government following the success of Eisenstein's earlier film Strike (1924). The film dramatizes the 1905 mutiny aboard the Imperial Russian battleship Potemkin and the subsequent Cossack massacre of civilians in Odessa.

The film is structured in five acts. The first act, 'Men and Maggots,' shows the mutiny being triggered when sailors discover their meat/borscht is infested with maggots. A plate inscribed 'Give us this day our daily bread' is found or smashed after the discovery. A wild-haired priest bounces his cross into his palm while the doctor Smirnov is killed. Soldiers are about to fire on fellow sailors covered by a tarpaulin who refused the borscht, until they are convinced not to shoot. Sailor leader Vakulinchuk dies in the conflict and is laid in a tent on a pier. A man in the crowd shouts 'kill the Jews' and is beaten by those gathered around the tent. The only color in the entire film appears on a red flag.

The Odessa Steps sequence in Act IV is the film's most famous section and a foundational example of intellectual montage. Tsarist Cossack soldiers descend the steps massacring a crowd of civilians. The sequence features a baby carriage rolling uncontrolled down the steps; a nurse wearing pince-nez whose glasses are broken and who bleeds from her eye; a legless man crawling alongside the retreating mob; a woman carrying her trampled child back toward the soldiers; and three separate shots of stone lion statues edited together to give the illusion of the lions rising up. The sequence begins after the caption 'suddenly...' interrupts a shot of the waving crowd. The final shot of the whole film shows the battleship charging directly at the viewer, appearing to split the screen.

The Berlin cut of the film introduced composer Edmund Meisel's score. Eisenstein's montage theory categorized five types of montage: intellectual, overtonal, tonal, rhythmic, and metric; he developed these concepts alongside peers including Vsevolod Pudovkin. The film was a major influence on Francis Bacon, who painted 'Study for the Nurse in the Battleship Potemkin, 1957' and incorporated the screaming, bloody-eyed nurse image across his screaming popes series. Chris Marker's A Grin Without a Cat opens with footage from the film spliced with protest footage. Brian De Palma paid direct homage to the Odessa Steps sequence in The Untouchables, moving a shootout to Chicago's Union Station with a pram rolling down stairs. Terry Gilliam parodied the sequence in Brazil by substituting a vacuum cleaner for the baby carriage. Romanian director Radu Jude engaged with the film in two works: a short (The Potemkinists) about a sculptor making a canal-side monument to the film's protagonists, and a 2022 feature that freeze-frames the film's final scene of men waving their hats.
recursive_suggestions: Sergei Eisenstein — director; owns Strike, October, Alexander Nevsky; rich in quizbowl clues (Fine Arts)
Francis Bacon — his screaming popes series directly references the Battleship Potemkin nurse image (Fine Arts)
Odessa Steps — the scene may have its own answerline entries (Fine Arts)

Film: a wild-haired priest bounces his cross into his palm while doctor Smirnov is killed; soldiers covering sailors under a tarpaulin are convinced not to shoot; the only color in the silent film is on a red flag
Film: directed by Sergei Eisenstein with cinematography by Eduard Tisse; commissioned after the success of his earlier film Strike (1924); the Berlin cut introduced Edmund Meisel's score
